coredata.dc:title: Exploring optoelectronic properties of undoped and amine-doped carbon dots: Impact of surface functionalization and pH
coredata.dc:creator: Panigrahi, Aradhana
Behera, Ranjan Kumar
Mishra, Leepsa
Kumar, Satish
Dubey, Priyanka
Dutta, Soumi
Sarangi, Manas Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Tuning optoelectronic properties through surface modifications and doping in low dimensional carbon dots (CDs) has been an asset in designing them for versatile applications. In this article, we explore the modulations in photophysical and charge transport characteristics of doped and undoped CDs functionalized with redox-active dopamine (DA). The pH-dependent DA chemistry on the surface of CDs is observed to have deterring effect on excited state dynamics with superior charge transfer efficiency for pristine CDs compared with the doped one. Charge transport studies through conducting measurements across electrode-CDs-electrode junction report a pH-induced current enhancement with DA functionalization resulting from a shifted equilibrium among the reduced (hydroquinone) and oxidized (quinone) isomers of DA. While the changes in the optical and electronic features are significantly perturbed with pH for the DA-treated undoped CDs, nominal alterations in spectral features are reported for the doped ones. The current-voltage conductance across the junctions is influenced by surface-treated CDs for acidic pH, with signatures of direct tunneling for a low bias regime, curtailing the Fowler-Nordheim (FN) tunneling. These features can have immediate optoelectronic-based applications in pH-based sensors and molecular electronics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dopamine
Carbon dots
Surface functionalization
Electron transfer
Current sensing atomic force microscope

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM micrographs of CDs (a), ACDs (b), and their size distribution (c). The average size of CDs and ACDs are 4 and 3.8 nm, respectively. FTIR spectra of CDs and ACDs (d), UV–visible absorption (dashed line), excitation (dotted line) and PL spectra (solid line) of CDs and ACDs (e), and the corresponding lifetime decay curves of CDs (red) and ACDs (magenta) (f) at λex = 377 nm. The PL spectra are measured at λex = 370 nm and the excitation spectra are recorded at their respective emission maxima. The average lifetimes of CDs and ACDs are calculated to be 4.38 and 15.18 ns, respectively, by using Equation (2) . (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Absorption (dashed line), excitation (dotted line), and PL emission spectra (solid line) of (a) CDs (0.2 mg/mL), (d) DCDs (0.03 mg/mL), and (c, f) lifetime decay curves of CDs and DCDs at λex = 377 nm respectively, PL emission spectra with a variation of the excitation wavelength from 280 to 450 nm of CDs (b), and DCDs (e), the inset of b shows the respective color of the wavelengths from 280 to 450 nm and the inset of e plots comparison between the PL emission spectra of CDs and DCDs at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. Here, CDs and DCDs are shown in black and purple (pH 3), red and pink (pH 7), and blue and olive (pH 10), respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Absorption (dashed line), excitation (dotted line), and PL emission spectra (solid line) of (a) ACDs (0.2 mg/mL), (d) DACDs (0.03 mg/mL), and (c, f) lifetime decay curves of ACDs and DACDs at λex = 377 nm respectively, PL emission spectra with varying the excitation wavelength from 280 to 450 nm of CDs (b), and DACDs (e), the inset of b presents the respective color of the wavelengths from 280 to 450 and the resemblance in the emission spectra of ACDs and DACDs at λex = 370 nm is plotted in the inset of e. Colors corresponding to ACDs and DACDs at pH 3, 7, and 10 are cyan and wine, magenta and violet, navy and dark cyan, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Comparison of I–V curves of CDs (a), DCDs (b), ACDs (c), and DACDs (d) at pH 3, 7, and 10. Briefly, (a) shows the I–V curves for CDs in the circle (black for pH 3, red for pH 7, blue for pH 10), (b) for DCDs in the up triangle (purple for pH 3, pink for pH 7, olive for pH 10), (c) for ACDs in the sphere (cyan for pH 3, magenta for pH 7, navy for pH 10), and (d) for DACDs in the square (wine for pH 3, purple for pH 7, dark cyan for pH 10). The I–V curves at lower bias are fitted to linear equation ( Equation (8a) , dark yellow color), and the higher bias regions are fitted with FN equation ( Equation (8b) , corresponding color of the I–V curves). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

FN plots for CDs (a), DCDs (b), ACDs (c), and DACDs (d) at pH 3, 7, and 10. The CDs are represented by the circle (black for pH 3, red for pH 7, blue for pH 10), DCDs by the up triangle (purple for pH 3, pink for pH 7, olive for pH 10), ACDs by the sphere (cyan for pH 3, magenta for pH 7, navy for pH 10), and DACDs by the square (wine for pH 3, violet for pH 7, dark cyan for pH 10). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The feasible mechanism of DA functionalization on the surface of CDs in the presence of two linkers, EDC and NHS (a), ET process between CDs and DA in different pH media (b).

Schematic illustration of (a) dependence of electrical conductance of all the nanodots on solvent pH and (b) barrier shapes in eV<φ and eV>φ regimes for direct and FN tunneling, respectively. The conductance is maximum in acidic pH followed by alkaline pH and minimum in neutral pH medium for all samples.
Average lifetime values of CDs, DCDs, ACDs, and DACDs at pH 3, 7, and 10, fitted using Equation (1) and calculated using Equation (2) .

head.ce:title: Exploring optoelectronic properties of undoped and amine-doped carbon dots: Impact of surface functionalization and pH

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology, Patna, 801106, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology, Patna, 801106, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tuning optoelectronic properties through surface modifications and doping in low dimensional carbon dots (CDs) has been an asset in designing them for versatile applications. In this article, we explore the modulations in photophysical and charge transport characteristics of doped and undoped CDs functionalized with redox-active dopamine (DA). The pH-dependent DA chemistry on the surface of CDs is observed to have deterring effect on excited state dynamics with superior charge transfer efficiency for pristine CDs compared with the doped one. Charge transport studies through conducting measurements across electrode-CDs-electrode junction report a pH-induced current enhancement with DA functionalization resulting from a shifted equilibrium among the reduced (hydroquinone) and oxidized (quinone) isomers of DA. While the changes in the optical and electronic features are significantly perturbed with pH for the DA-treated undoped CDs, nominal alterations in spectral features are reported for the doped ones. The current-voltage conductance across the junctions is influenced by surface-treated CDs for acidic pH, with signatures of direct tunneling for a low bias regime, curtailing the Fowler-Nordheim (FN) tunneling. These features can have immediate optoelectronic-based applications in pH-based sensors and molecular electronics.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum confined low dimensional carbon dots (CDs) exhibit fascinating optical and electronic properties that can be tuned with varied sizes, compositions, functionalizations, etc. [1]. Over the last decade, CDs have gained much recognition due to their easy low-cost synthesis, low toxicity, photostability, and excellent water solubility, and have found wide applications in sensing, biomedicine, catalysis, optoelectronics, etc. [2–5] With the abundance and options of dangling surface ligands, both optical and electronic properties of CDs can be altered with the immediate changes in the local environment, functionalization, and doping along with the presence of adjacent molecules like dyes, redox molecules, metals, etc. [6–8]. These alterations originate from photo-induced charge or energy transfer between CDs with nearby interacting molecules and are often considered critical for their device-based optoelectronic performances [9]. In this work, we attempt to decipher the photoinduced charge transfer dynamics of CDs with the neurotransmitter dopamine (DA). DA is an important catecholamine found naturally in the human body and acts as a neurotransmitter for signal transport in the central nervous system [10,11]. The motivation behind such selection stems from the fact that the optical properties of DA are prone to changes in surrounding pH [12,13], and have been reported to act both as an electron donor and acceptor when functionalized or doped with nanoparticles [14,15]. In acidic pH, DA remains in its hydroquinone (HQN) form, however, in the alkaline medium, it switches to its quinone (QN) isomer. With a special interest in sensing and bioimaging both in vitro and in vivo, several attempts have been made to explore the exciting redox properties of DA with fluorescent quantum dots (QDs) [14–18]. For example, Ji et al. have used CdSe/ZnS QDs and DA conjugates to study the effect of DA and pH on the photoluminescence (PL) quenching properties of QDs [16]. With an increase in pH from 4 to 10, they observe a sequential drop in both PL intensity and the average lifetime and assigned it to the photoinduced charge transfer interaction between QDs and DA. In a pioneering work, Medintz et al. have reported that QD-DA-peptide bioconjugates can act as a charge transfer-assisted pH sensor for measuring changes in cytoplasmic pH. The oxidized DA in its QN form is observed to be an efficient electron acceptor from QDs, opening the possibility of non-radiative channels for quenching of the QDs [15].

While the choice of any random nanoparticle can have a detrimental toxic effect, CDs qualify as a natural option due to their low toxicity, excellent water solubility, biocompatibility, and cell penetration, and has been used as a reporter for sensing applications in vivo [18–23]. Chai et al. have reported a biocompatible DA functionalized carbon QDs conjugate for the detection of intracellular tyrosinase activity, where photoinduced electron transfer from QDs to dopaquinone in an oxidative form is explained to be the key driving mechanism [18]. The doping of CDs has also been found to be effective in influencing the charge transfer dynamics and has been reported to deter the PL quantum yield [24,25]. Lei et al. have designed, and synthesized DA functionalized S, N-co-doped CDs (DA-S, N-CDs) by covalently linking DA to the S, N-CDs surface through an EDC/NHS coupling reaction for the detection of Fe3+ and Fe2+ in water with the detection limit of 2.85 μM and 2.06 μM [24]. Investigations on the redox chemistry of DA attached to QDs guide that the variation of pH leading to the formation of reduced (HQN) to oxidized (QN) isomers can severely deter the emissive behavior of the QDs through charge transfer. With an emphasis on charge transfer at a molecular level, in this article, we attempt to explore the pH-dependent redox active interactions of DA with doped and functionalized CDs for investigating the alterations not just to their optical characteristics but also to their electronic transport properties. With the advancement in molecular devices and versatile applications in nanoscale electronic circuits, the regulatory role of pH in charge transport (CTp) characteristic across the junctions deserve thorough investigation. Li et al. report a single-molecule-based pH-induced switching in conductance across a metal-molecule-metal device with a scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) break junction technique [26]. The dye-mediated (malachite green) high on/off ratio in the device conductivity (100:1) is achieved by the reversible structural transformation between the conjugated and unconjugated states of the malachite by inducting a pH switching from 5.5 to 13.6. CTp variation in pH-induced protonated and deprotonated pyridine derivatives through quantum interference is studied by Tang et al. [27]. With flicker noise analysis, they report a through-bond higher electronic conductance for the protonated pyridinium compared to the through-space electron conductance for the pyridine across the molecular junction. The effect of pH on the morphology and conductivity of polyaniline films is reported by Hong et al. [28]. They have concluded that the films prepared under acidic conditions show more conductivity than that prepared at higher pH suggesting that the degree of protonation during preparation is very crucial for conductivity calculation.

With a series of spectroscopic and current sensing atomic force microscopic (CS-AFM) measurements, we intend to decipher the pH-induced optical and electronic charge transfer behavior of pristine and doped CDs with DA functionalization. Like STM, CS-AFM is a high-resolution single molecule based current-voltage (I–V) measurement technique that decifers electrical properties along with surface topography. The I–V measurements across the electrode-QD-electrode junction in CS-AFM can often be perturbed by the nature of the electrode, doping, density of states (DOS), electrostatic interactions, presence of electron donor or acceptor moieties, surface treatment, pH, etc [8,29–31]. With steady-state and time-resolved PL measurements, we observe that the photophysics of DA-attached CDs (DCDs), reflect pH-dependent optical properties compared to CDs. Unlike CDs, amine-doped CDs (ACDs) show similar pH-induced photophysical signatures irrespective of DA attachment (DACDs). However, CS-AFM studies reflect the critical role of surface treatment on the I–V measurements with functionalized CDs showing more conductivity compared to pristine ones. Moreover, with an alteration in the environmental pH, the conductivity increases considerably for acidic pH for all the samples which can directly tunnel through the potential barrier at low bias voltage as compared to neutral and alkaline pH.

Citric acid (CA, ≥ 99.5%), hydrochloric acid (HCl, about 35%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥ 97%) are bought from Merck and ethylenediamine (EDA, ≥ 99%), N-(3-(dimethylamino) propyl)-N′-ethyl carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, ≥ 97%), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, ≥ 98%), and dopamine hydrochloride are bought from Sigma Aldrich, used without additional purification. Deionized (DI) water is taken as a solvent for performing all the experiments.

Both undoped and amine-doped CDs are synthesized using the pyrolysis method, where CA is the initial precursor for the carbon source and EDA is used as a nitrogen source for the doped one. 1 g of CA added in DI water (2 mL) and heated for 4 h at 250 °C with the addition of DI water in between. An orange-coloured sticky sample is obtained, which affirms the formation of CDs. ACDs are prepared using a similar process as that of CDs followed by pyrolyzing CA for one more hour with EDA. The obtained brown color viscous solution confirms the formation of ACDs. Both the CDs and ACDs are purified by using column chromatography with ethanol and stored for further use.

DCDs and DACDs are synthesized by using the following method as reported previously [17]. Briefly, into a beaker containing 100 mL of 10 g/L NaOH solution, the obtained CDs and ACDs are added dropwise under vigorous stirring (25 °C, 1000 rpm). After neutralizing its pH to 7, 4 mg of NHS and 3 mg of EDC are added into 10 mL of the as-prepared aqueous solution of CDs/ACDs at room temperature under vigorous stirring (1000 rpm). Then 2 mg of DA hydrochloride is added to the above solution and the reaction is continued for 2 h. Now, the DCDs and DACDs are prepared and preserved for further use.

The PL excitation along with emission and absorption spectra are measured by a Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer and JASCO V-770 spectrophotometer, respectively. Both measurements are performed using quartz cuvettes with a 10 mm path length. All the experiments are performed at three different pH's, i.e., pH 3, 7, and 10. pH 3 and 10 are obtained by using concentrated HCl and NaOH respectively. A PerkinElmer Spectrum 400 spectrometer is used to record the Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectra of the CDs and ACDs before and after DA, in attenuated total reflection (ATR) mode. All the measurements are conducted at room temperature.

PL lifetime measurements are determined using a picosecond pulsed diode laser (λex = 377 nm) and microchannel plate – photomultiplier tube (MCP-PMT) detector-based Horiba time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) spectrometer. The details of the instrument are described elsewhere [32–35]. Briefly, the recorded data are analyzed and fitted with multiexponential function, after deconvoluting the Instrument Response Function (IRF) using EzTime 3.2.9.6 data analysis software ( Equation (1)
). The full-width half maximum of IRF is observed to be 270 ps with a channel resolution of 28 ps/channel. The fitting parameter χ2 is measured as an indicator for best fit. The estimated error is found to be less than 10% of the measured value.

I
( t )
=
∑
i = 1

− t
τ i

The average PL decay lifetime values are determined by using the formula,
(2)
τ
a v g
=
∑
i = 1
n
A i
τ i 2
∑
i = 1
n
A i
τ i
Here, n = 3 for CDs and n = 2 for DCDs, ACDs, and DACDs.
Where, Ai's are the contributions corresponding to the lifetime components τi's, respectively, and τavg is the average lifetime of these QDs.

The radiative (k
r ) and non-radiative decay rate (k
nr ) constants are calculated using the formulas,
(3)

=

where, QY is the PL quantum yield of CDs (0.08) and ACDs (0.33) collected from previously reported articles [5,36].

The ET efficiency between CDs/ACDs and DA can be calculated as,
(5)
η
E T
= 1 −
τ
D A
τ 0
where τ0 and τDA represents the average lifetime of CDs and ACDs without and with DA attachment respectively.

Using a JEOL-F200 TEM operated at 200 kV, the TEM micrographs of CDs and ACDs are obtained. The samples are prepared by diluting CDs and ACDs in DI water, followed by sonication for 5 min. Then the samples are drop-cast on copper grids coated with carbon and dried under vacuum. The TEM images are analyzed using ImageJ software.

Using the non-contact mode of AFM, topography images of CDs and ACDs are obtained using an Agilent Technologies 5500 model. The preparation method for samples is described below. We have taken silicon wafers as the substrate for non-contact imaging. First, the silicon substrates are cleaned with acetone followed by sonication for 5 min followed by DI water. The diluted samples of CDs and ACDs are spin-coated on silicon substrates at 500 rpm for 60 s. All the samples for imaging are prepared at ambient environmental conditions and the AFM images are interpreted using WSxM 5.0 developer 8.3 software.

The contact mode is used to record all the CS-AFM measurements with a Pt-coated probe. For the conducting measurements, CDs, DCDs, ACDs, and DACDs are spin-coated over highly oriented pyrolytic graphite (HOPG). First, HOPG layers are peeled off and pasted on a glass substrate with double-faced tape. The spin coating technique is used for the deposition of diluted samples spinning for 1 min at 500 rpm and dried overnight. The electrical measurements have been recorded with biasing voltage sweeping from −2 V to +2 V for forward bias and +2 V to −2 V for reverse bias. The I–V data have been analyzed with Origin 8.5 software.

Various tunneling models are adopted to analyze the transport mechanism [37] as described below.
i)
Schottky Emission:

Schottky equation with applied voltage (V) and the tunneling current (I) can be represented as below,

In this article, we have investigated the optical properties of the as-synthesized carbon QDs such as CDs, and ACDs with and without DA attachment. The characterization is accomplished with multiple microscopic (upper panel of Fig. 1 ) and spectroscopic (lower panel of Fig. 1) methods. Fig. 1a–c represent the TEM micrographs and the size distribution of CDs and ACDs, respectively, indicating their spherical morphology. A similar structure is also confirmed by AFM topographic images (Fig. S1). The average diameters obtained from the Gaussian fitting of the size distribution for CDs and ACDs are found to be ∼4 nm and ∼3.8 nm, respectively (Fig. 1c). These values are in line with earlier literature reports on CDs and ACDs [8]. The functionalizations of the CDs and ACDs are tested with FTIR measurements (Fig. 1d) in ATR mode. The pristine CDs show peaks at ∼3315 cm−1 and ∼1636 cm−1, which are ascribed to the –OH stretching and CO stretching vibrations respectively, confirming the presence of the carboxylic group [8,17]. The nitrogen doping by EDA is confirmed by the presence of amide groups in ACDs from the peaks at 1388 cm−1, 1350 cm−1, 1256 cm−1 (C–N stretching), and 1646 cm−1 (CO stretching) [36]. The normalized steady-state absorption spectra of CDs (dashed red line in Fig. 1e) reflect two characteristics peaks at ∼205 nm and ∼340 nm, assigned to the π-π* transition of the aromatic CC bond and the n-π* transition of the CO bond. The band between 300 nm and 400 nm is attributed to the surface state transition of CDs [38,39]. ACDs exhibit alike band structures suggesting similar transition states for both (dashed magenta line in Fig. 1e). The normalized steady-state PL spectra of CDs (solid red line in Fig. 1e) and ACDs (solid magenta line in Fig. 1e) show emission maxima at 442 nm and 455 nm, respectively, displaying blue emission. The actual spectra (Fig. S1c) reflect that the PL quantum yield for ACD is higher than the CD. The broad absorption in these QDs drives us to measure the excitation spectra providing the precise excitation value at their corresponding emission maxima (dotted lines in Fig. 1e). The excitation maxima for CDs and ACDs are measured to be ∼360 nm and ∼375 nm, collected at their respective emission maxima. The time-resolved PL decay curves at the excitation wavelength 377 nm and the corresponding emission maxima of CDs and ACDs are shown in Fig. 1f. The decay curves of both CDs and ACDs are fitted with multiexponential functions as given in Equation (1) and the average lifetime values are determined using Equation (2) (Tables S1 and S2). It is noticed that doping in CDs increases the average decay lifetime of CDs from 4.38 ns to 15.18 ns, in line with the steady-state measurement. This is attributed to the appearance of additional surface states acting as the trap sites for the carriers, enhancing the radiative recombination in ACDs [40,41]. From these lifetime values and the QYs collected from previous reports, the radiative rate constants k
r and non-radiative rate constants k
nr values are estimated using Equations 3 and 4 , respectively [42]. For CDs, the k
r and k
nr values are calculated as 1.8 × 107 s−1 and 21.0 × 107 s−1 and for ACDs, these are 2.2 × 107 s−1 and 4.4 × 107 s−1, respectively (Table S3). The relative percentage of radiative recombination over non-radiative one (from the ratio k
r
/k
nr ) of ACDs is found to be much higher (50.0%) than the CDs (8.5%), indicating enhanced radiative recombination compared to the various accompanied non-radiative phenomena.

Fig. 3 represents the steady-state and time-resolved spectroscopic observation of ACDs at three different pH before and after DA attachment. It is evident that the nitrogen doping in ACDs is not able to perturb the absorption spectra (Fig. 3a, dashed line), however, it slightly influences the PL intensity at varied pH, with emission maxima at 455 nm (Fig. 3a, solid line). The excitation spectra (Fig. 3a, dotted line) also exhibit similar characteristics with maxima at ∼375 nm, irrespective of the pH. Moreover, the EIDPL behavior remains unchanged after DA attachment or pH variation (Fig. 3b and e, S8), suggesting less interaction between ACDs and DA. We have also observed a similar trend in the excited state where the maximum average lifetime for pH 7 is 15.18 ns, but for pH 10 and 3, the lifetimes are calculated as 14.75 ns and 12.71 ns, respectively (Fig. 3c, Table 1, S2). This may be due to the protonation of amine groups in a strongly acidic environment causing the accumulation of ACDs with a quenched PL intensity and lifetime [53,54].

Unlike DCDs, the DACDs do not show any modification in the PL spectra or decay profile as evident from the lower panel of Fig. 3, with a slight change in the absorption spectra. The absorption spectra of DACDs gives a similar feature in the blue region (∼280 nm), while no signature of ACDs is found (dashed line in Fig. 3d, Fig. S2b). This suggests a weaker DA attachment on the surface of ACDs. The pH variation to the DACDs results in no major change in the emission peak at 455 nm (solid line) and excitation maximum at ∼375 nm (dotted line) as given in Fig. 3d. Like DCDs, the EIDPL feature of the ACDs is not hampered by the treatment of DA or pH change (Fig. 3e, S8). A comparative study of PL spectra of ACDs and DACDs with λex (370 nm) signifies no disparity in the emission peaks in any pH value (inset of Fig. 3e, Fig. S6b). Similar results are obtained from the time-resolved PL decay of DACDs with very minute change in the lifetime values compared to ACDs from 15.18 ns to 15.06 ns at pH 7, 12.71 ns–12.64 ns at pH 3, and 14.75 ns–14.69 ns at pH 10 (Fig. 3f, Table 1, S5). The comparative λex vs. λem plot for CDs, DCDs, ACDs, and DACDs is shown in Fig. S6c, which reveals that the emissive profiles for CDs and DCDs are completely different, while that for ACDs and DACDs remain almost identical. This is indicative of a weak ET efficiency between DA and ACDs resulting from the weak attachment between ACDs and DA. The η
ET value for pH 3, 7, and 10 are 0.6%, 0.8%, and 0.4% respectively showing a negligible alteration with pH variation. Unlike the DCDs, the FTIR spectra of DACDs (Fig. S9, violet) do not exhibit any additional and prominent peaks in comparison to ACDs, implying a less probability of DA attachment on ACDs surface, which is in line with our optical results.

Further, such interaction and chemical bond formation between CDs/ACDs and DA are explained in terms of their surface charges. According to previous literature, the zeta potential distribution of CDs and ACDs suggests a negatively charged surface for CDs, while a positively charged surface prevails for ACDs [8]. Jodko-Piorecka et al. have reported that the DA itself has a positive surface, which is prone to electrostatic interaction with anionic lipid head groups [55]. Literature reports on DA suggest that the cationic nature of DA is due to the presence of the amine group [56,57]. Previous reports have described electrostatic Coulombic interaction between charged surfaces of CDs/ACDs and interacting molecules as an indicator of PL quenching. The electrostatic attraction between the anionic graphene QDs and cationic DA is explained to be beneficial for the enhanced sensitivity for the detection of DA as reported by Huang et al. [58]. In another report, Hutton et al. have revealed an efficient ET between positively charged ammonium-terminated CDs and negatively charged enzymes while a reduced ET is monitored for negatively charged carboxylate-terminated CDs due to weak electrostatic interaction [59] Herein, the cationic DA can have Coulombic attraction with the negatively charged surfaces of CDs which assist them to come into proximity, enhancing the possibility of forming DCDs (Scheme 1 a). On the other hand, the electrostatic repulsion between the cationic DA and the positively charged ACDs inhibits such chemical bonds leading to less probable DACDs. This results in a significant η
ET of 58.7% for CDs compared to only 0.8% for ACDs. The DA attachment to CDs encourages the ET process between the two (Scheme 1b). On the other hand, weak interaction between DA and ACDs makes the ET less likely (Scheme S2).

The ensemble steady-state and time-resolved spectroscopic studies elucidate that both DA functionalization and the impact of change in pH modulate their optical behaviors. Though the photoinduced optical change directs to a charge transfer between DA and CDs at different pH, a supplementing conductance measurement across electrode-QD-electrode junction often establishes their impact on the optoelectronic application. As discussed in the introduction, CS-AFM warrants this with simultaneous visualization of the topography and the local conductance of the nanoscale sample surface and is ideal for unveiling the electrical transport across a single molecular junction [31,60]. We have attempted the current measurement as a function of the applied voltage across CDs and ACDs deposited over the HOPG substrate serving as an electrode along with a platinum AFM tip as the second electrode. Under identical experimental conditions, typical CS-AFM I–V characteristics for CD, and ACD (with and without DA attachment) at varied pH are shown in Fig. 4 a-d. A control measurement only on HOPG without the sample shows Ohmic-type conduction as shown in Fig. S10g. To check the reproducibility of the I–V characteristics, we have repeated the measurement 20 times on each sample for the entire bias range (Fig. S10 for CDs and DCDs and Fig. S11 for ACDs and DACDs), reflecting that the I–V features are traceable. A comparative analysis of I–V traces at different pH suggests that irrespective of the nature of attachment or doping, conductivity for the acidic or basic pH is higher than the neutral medium (Scheme 2 a). We attribute the enhancement in the electrical conductivity of the QDs to the increased number of charge carriers in acidic (protons) and basic (hydroxides) media, resulting in the different dangling structures on the surface. Surface redox treatment with reducing and oxidizing agents have been reported to change the DOS at the Fermi level with the introduction of additional charge carriers and hence resulting in improved conductivity [5,61,62]. Tang et al. have reported that the addition of acid to M3, leading to protonated M3-H increases the conductivity, while the introduction of the base forming the deprotonated M3 leads to a drop in conductivity, and hence acts as a reversible switching device [27]. In a pH variation study on the ionic nano-porous device, Kalman et al. accomplished similar chemical control over the I–V features by regulating the surface charges on the pore walls in a bipolar transistor with on/off switching applications [63]. Our results are in line with previous literature reports, where similar tuning in the conductivity is observed with variation in the concentration of protons in the media. To test the influence of surface attachment, a comparative analysis of DA-attachment to the pristine CDs/ACDs infers further increase in conductivity for all the varied pH (Figs. S12 and S13). These results resemble our steady-state and time-resolved PL measurements. The probable factors influencing the conductivity after DA functionalization are bandgap reduction and the increase in DOS by introducing new surface states [8,40,50]. The modulation in the DOS at the Fermi level after DA attachment generates additional charge carriers causing a current amplification [5,61]. This is verified by the DOS measurements derived from the numerical derivative of I–V (dI/dV) curves, as shown in Fig. S14. Both CDs and ACDs after DA attachment exhibit distinct behavior with smaller electronic bandgap energies (Eg) compared to the pristine CDs and ACDs (Table S6). The increment in charge carrier with attachment is ascribed to the facile medium-dependent double electron and protonation equilibrium between the QN and HQN form of DA [14,15] (Scheme 1a). Darwish et al. have reported a proton-coupled electron transfer equilibrium between the reduced and oxidized form of an anthraquinone derivative 5AQ5 and hypothesized better quantum interference in their structure as a reason for the conductivity enhancement [64]. The cross-conjugated QNs in an HQN/QN system undergo destructive interference lowering its conductance, unlike the linearly conjugated HQN [64–66]. As described in the I–V measurements for DCDs and DACDs, the HQN form at acidic pH displays a higher conductance than the QN form of DA (Fig. 4b and d). It is noteworthy to mention here that upon insertion of DA with CDs and ACDs, we observe a much higher increment in the conductance for DCD compared to insignificant changes in DACD (Figs. S12 and S13). We assign this to a much stronger attachment of DA to CDs compared to the weaker interaction with ACDs, arising due to structural differences in the interfacial dangling bond. It is well documented that current conduction through a chemically bonded structure is faster compared to the through-space current, with significantly low resistance for the bonded structure [30]. This is in line with our FTIR signatures indicating that a stronger bonding is plausible for the DCDs compared to that of the weaker bonding for DACDs.

To segregate both the tunneling mechanisms, we have further analyzed the above data by re-plotting it with the semi-logarithmic plot as ln(I/V2) ∼ V−1, the FN plot, as shown in Fig. 5 from the I–V curves by using Equations 9a and 9b . The inflection point (transition voltage, V
trans ) separates the plot into two regimes of higher bias one with a linear decay for FN tunneling and a lower bias range with exponential growth for direct tunneling [67,69]. Vtrans between the direct and FN parts, indicates a conversion from a trapezoidal to a triangular barrier when the bias voltage is raised over the barrier height. Table S7 represents the voltage regime where we observe a significant difference in V
trans for DCDs with pH variation. The neutral medium shows a V
trans value of 0.45 V, while a pH change to 3 or 10 decreases this value to 0.39 V. However, minimal alteration is noticed for CDs with V
trans values of 0.47 V, 0.49 V, 0.48 V for pH 3, 7, 10, respectively. In addition, the ACDs and DACDs display similar behavior with lower V
trans values at pH 3 (0.33 V, 0.31 V) than 7 and 10 (both 0.41 V, 0.37 V), respectively. These outcomes are in good agreement with our conductance measurements in CDs and ACDs with DA attachments. This suggests that doping, DA attachment, and pH variation can influence the transport from a direct (trapezoidal) to FN (triangular) type tunneling compared to the pristine CDs.

We have elucidated the role of DA surface functionalization and the solvent pH on the optoelectronic features of undoped and doped CDs. With a series of spectroscopic and conductance measurements, we infer that the photoinduced charge transfer and transport are driven by the pH-dependent redox properties of the attached DA. The charge transfer efficiency in DCDs is found to be enhanced in the alkaline medium, with the DA-QN acting as an electron acceptor. Not just spectroscopic signatures, I–V traces for DA-treated undoped CDs across electrode-QD-electrode are also perturbed with pH, however, the features for the amine-doped CDs are observed to be weakly interactive. The higher conductivity in the acidic medium arises due to changes in the number of charge carriers across the junction with a change in the nature of the I–V traces from FN tunneling to direct one. We anticipate that these distinct changes in the features with surface modifications and doping can be harnessed for engineering devices in sensing-based optoelectronic applications.

Aradhana Panigrahi: Conceptualization, Design, Synthesis, Execution, MethodologyMethodology, Spectroscopic and CS-AFM measurements, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Ranjan Kumar Behera: Conceptualization, Design, Synthesis, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Leepsa Mishra: MethodologyMethodology, Formal analysis, CS-AFM analysis, Schematic presentation, Writing – original draft, discussions. Satish Kumar: CS-AFM analysis, Writing – original draft and discussions, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Priyanka Dubey: Writing – original draft and discussions, Writing – original draft. Soumi Dutta: Writing – original draft and discussions, Writing – original draft. Manas Kumar Sarangi: Conceptualization, Design, Writing – review & editing, Execution, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the funding received from the science and engineering research board (SERB) through project CRG/2022/004870. AP, PD, and SD thank IIT Patna for the research fellowship. The authors acknowledge Mr. Nishant Kumar and Ms. Itishree Pradhan for helping with CS-AFM measurements.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.S. Sonders, S.-J. Zhu, N.R. Zahniser, M.P. Kavanaugh, S.G. Amara, Multiple Ionic Conductances of the Human Dopamine Transporter: The Actions of Dopamine and Psychostimulants, J. Neurosci. 17(3) (1997) 960-974.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.L. Berfield, L.C. Wang, M.E.A. Reith, Which Form of Dopamine Is the Substrate for the Human Dopamine Transporter: the Cationic or the Uncharged Species? *, J. Biol. Chem. 274(8) (1999) 4876-4882.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G.A.M. Hutton, B. Reuillard, B.C.M. Martindale, C.A. Caputo, C.W.J. Lockwood, J.N. Butt, E. Reisner, Carbon Dots as Versatile Photosensitizers for Solar-Driven Catalysis with Redox Enzymes, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 138(51) (2016) 16722-16730.
